does: This paper identifies and systematically exploits a vulnerability in error handling loops of tool-calling systems within the Model Context Protocol. The authors present VATS, a mutation-driven framework that generates adversarial payloads across seven structural and linguistic dimensions to inject malicious instructions through tool error messages. Testing against four frontier models (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.5, GLM-5.1, Qwen3-Coder), they demonstrate that error-path injection achieves up to triple the success rate of standard indirect prompt injection attacks, reaching 100% compliance rates in controlled settings. The key finding is that structural positioning—embedding adversarial instructions within error message contexts—emerges as the most effective exploit vector. The work shows that while framework-level guardrails can provide some mitigation, models remain fundamentally susceptible at the base layer, creating systemic risks for autonomous agent deployments.
connects: This work addresses a gap in security analysis of the emerging Model Context Protocol standardization, building on the established literature of prompt injection attacks and adversarial robustness in language models. It extends prior research on indirect prompt injection by identifying error handling as an underexplored attack surface that models treat with implicit authority. The systematic mutation-based approach draws from fuzzing and adversarial search methodologies common in software security and ML adversarial examples research. The paper responds to growing concerns about autonomous agent safety as tool-calling becomes standardized, connecting to prior work on agent alignment and the security implications of increased model autonomy. It leverages insights from studies on how models distinguish between user inputs and system messages, showing that error contexts exploit this distinction to bypass safety heuristics.
enables: This research points toward several future directions in securing autonomous agent systems. It motivates the development of more robust error-handling mechanisms that resist adversarial injection while maintaining system functionality. The work suggests needs for better detection systems that identify when models enter corrective reasoning modes triggered by adversarial error messages, and for techniques that help models maintain consistent security postures across different input modalities. The systematic mutation framework itself could enable broader red-teaming efforts across emerging protocol standards. More broadly, the findings highlight the necessity of hardening model-layer defenses rather than relying solely on framework-level mitigations. This sets up future work on provably secure error handling in agent systems, better understanding of why error contexts reduce model robustness, and development of training approaches that maintain safety guarantees across all input channels.
